topology.type: The storage platform determines available protection levels, efficiency formulas, and feature support. Each platform has different overhead models and management tools.
topology.level: The protection level controls how many drive failures the array can survive, and how much capacity is consumed by redundancy. Higher protection means lower usable capacity but better resilience.
topology.hotSpares: Hot spares are idle drives that automatically replace failed drives, reducing rebuild time. They reserve capacity but improve resilience. Not needed for platforms with distributed spare capacity (e.g., ADAPT, dRAID).

hardware.connectivity: Drive interface determines maximum per-drive throughput. NVMe offers the highest bandwidth (up to 7 GB/s per drive). SAS and SATA are limited to ~2.4 GB/s and ~0.6 GB/s respectively.
hardware.formFactor: Physical form factor affects density and cooling. 3.5" drives offer maximum HDD capacity. 2.5" and newer form factors (E3.S, EDSFF) are optimized for SSDs and NVMe.
hardware.driveModel: Individual drive specifications affect all calculations: capacity, IOPS, throughput, power consumption, endurance (DWPD for SSDs), and cost.
hardware.driveCount: Drives per server. Combined with server count, this determines total raw capacity and aggregate performance. More drives increase rebuild time for parity-based topologies.
hardware.serverCount: Number of servers or nodes. Affects total drive count, power consumption, and for distributed platforms (vSAN, Ceph, S2D, Nutanix), determines fault domain count and minimum host requirements.
hardware.serverPower: Base server power consumption (excluding drives). Used to calculate total energy, annual cost, and CO₂ emissions.

workload.readWrite: Read/write ratio significantly impacts effective IOPS. RAID 5/6 writes incur a penalty (4x for RAID-5, 6x for RAID-6) because each write requires reading old data, old parity, writing new data, and new parity.
workload.randomSequential: Random I/O is measured in IOPS; sequential I/O is measured in throughput (MB/s). Databases are typically 80%+ random; video streaming is nearly 100% sequential.
workload.blockSize: I/O block size affects both performance and alignment. Smaller blocks (4K-8K) maximize IOPS for databases. Larger blocks (128K-1M) maximize throughput for sequential workloads.
workload.datasetSize: Total amount of data to store. Used to calculate whether the configured storage provides enough usable capacity after all overheads.
workload.dailyWriteVolume: Daily write volume determines SSD endurance requirements (DWPD). High write volumes on SSDs can exceed the drive's rated endurance, leading to premature failure.

advanced.compression: Compression ratio multiplies effective capacity. Applied after usable capacity calculation. A 2x ratio doubles effective capacity. Compression is multiplicative with deduplication, not additive.
advanced.dedup: Deduplication ratio multiplies effective capacity by eliminating duplicate data blocks. Multiplicative with compression (e.g., 2x compression + 2x dedup = 4x effective capacity, not 3x).
advanced.networkSpeed: Network speed can become the bottleneck for distributed storage systems. For replication-based systems (vSAN, Ceph, Nutanix), writes must traverse the network to reach replica nodes.
advanced.pcieGen: PCIe generation determines the bus bandwidth per lane. Gen3: ~1 GB/s/lane, Gen4: ~2 GB/s/lane, Gen5: ~4 GB/s/lane. Combined with lane count, this sets the bus bandwidth ceiling.
advanced.pcieLanes: PCIe lanes multiplied by per-lane bandwidth gives total bus throughput. x4 is standard for NVMe drives, x8/x16 for RAID controllers and HBAs.
advanced.pue: Power Usage Effectiveness: ratio of total facility power to IT equipment power. PUE 1.0 means no cooling overhead. PUE 1.4 means 40% extra power for cooling. World average is ~1.55; best data centers achieve ~1.1.
advanced.fsType: Filesystem choice affects overhead and features. ZFS has ~3% slop space overhead. XFS/ReFS support reflinks for efficient snapshots. Btrfs supports snapshots with ~4% overhead.
advanced.controller: Storage controller or HBA limits total IOPS and throughput. Software RAID uses CPU for parity calculations. Hardware RAID controllers have fixed IOPS/throughput ceilings.
advanced.backupRetention: Number of days to keep backup copies. Combined with daily change rate, determines total backup storage needed.
advanced.dailyChangeRate: Percentage of data that changes each day. Higher change rates mean larger incremental backups and more storage consumption over the retention period.
advanced.performanceThreshold: Performance threshold limits how full the storage can be before degradation. Most storage systems slow down as they approach capacity. Setting 85% means only 85% of usable capacity is shown as the operational limit. Default 100% (no limit).

output.rawCapacity: Total physical capacity of all drives before any overhead. Calculated as: drive capacity x drive count x server count.
output.usableCapacity: Capacity available after subtracting parity/redundancy, hot spares, filesystem overhead, and system reserves. This is what you can actually write to.
output.effectiveCapacity: Usable capacity multiplied by compression and deduplication ratios. Represents the logical data volume the system can store.
output.efficiency: Ratio of usable capacity to raw capacity, expressed as a percentage. Higher is better, but higher efficiency usually means less redundancy.
output.sankeyDiagram: The Sankey diagram shows the capacity waterfall: how raw capacity flows through various overhead deductions to arrive at usable capacity. Wider flows represent larger quantities.
output.readThroughput: Maximum sequential read throughput, limited by the weakest link in the chain: media aggregate, controller/HBA, PCIe bus, or network.
output.writeThroughput: Maximum sequential write throughput. For parity-based RAID, write throughput is reduced by the write penalty factor.
output.readIops: Maximum random read IOPS. Sum of all drive read IOPS, limited by controller and bus ceilings.
output.writeIops: Maximum random write IOPS. For RAID-5, divide by 4 (each write needs 4 I/O operations). For RAID-6, divide by 6.
output.bottleneck: The bottleneck chain shows throughput at each layer. The layer with the lowest throughput is the bottleneck. Upgrading any other layer won't improve performance.
output.survivalRate: Probability of surviving one year without data loss, calculated via Monte Carlo simulation. Considers drive failure rates (AFR), rebuild time, URE probability during rebuild, and correlated failures.
output.rebuildTime: Estimated time to rebuild a failed drive. Longer rebuild times increase the window of vulnerability. Depends on drive capacity, rebuild speed, and array utilization.
output.totalPower: Total power consumption including drives, servers, and cooling (PUE factor). Used to calculate annual energy cost and CO₂ emissions.
output.annualCo2: Annual CO₂ emissions based on total energy consumption and the selected grid carbon intensity. Switzerland (30 g/kWh) is among the cleanest; China (555 g/kWh) among the highest.

zfs.ashift: The ashift value must match the drive's physical sector size. Using ashift=9 (512B) on a 4K-sector drive causes write amplification. Use ashift=12 for modern drives.
zfs.compression: ZFS compression algorithm. LZ4 is recommended for most workloads (fast, low CPU). ZSTD provides better ratios at higher CPU cost.
zfs.recordsize: Maximum record size for datasets. Match to workload: 128K for general files, 16K-64K for databases, 1M for large sequential workloads.
zfs.dedup: ZFS deduplication requires approximately 5 GB of RAM per TB of deduplicated data. Only enable if you have sufficient RAM and a write-heavy duplicate workload.
zfs.specialVdev: A special vdev (NVMe) stores metadata and small blocks, dramatically improving random I/O performance for HDD-based pools.

ceph.nearfull: Ceph's default nearfull threshold is 85%. OSDs will stop accepting writes above this threshold. Usable capacity is calculated at this threshold, not 100%.
ceph.backend: BlueStore is the recommended backend. It bypasses the local filesystem for direct disk access, improving performance over legacy FileStore.

vsan.adaptive: vSAN ESA uses adaptive RAID-5: 2+1 stripe width for 3-5 hosts (67% efficiency) or 4+1 for 6+ hosts (80% efficiency). The system automatically adjusts based on cluster size.

s2d.reserve: S2D reserves one drive equivalent of capacity per node for automatic rebuild. This reserve is subtracted before usable capacity is calculated.
s2d.map: Mirror-Accelerated Parity (MAP) combines a fast mirror tier (~20%) with a capacity-efficient parity tier (~80%), offering a balance of performance and efficiency.

nutanix.ec: Erasure Coding (EC-X): RF2 with EC(4/1) achieves ~80% efficiency. RF3 with EC(4/2) achieves ~67% efficiency. EC applies to cold data only; hot data remains replicated.
nutanix.rf: RF2 stores 2 copies of data (50% efficiency). RF3 stores 3 copies (33% efficiency). Higher RF means better fault tolerance but lower capacity.

netapp.wafl: NetApp WAFL filesystem has approximately 1.5% overhead for internal metadata. This is subtracted from usable capacity.
netapp.adp: Advanced Data Partitioning (ADP) shares drives between root and data aggregates, improving capacity utilization on smaller systems.
netapp.snapshot: Snapshot reserve (default 5%) is pre-allocated space for point-in-time copies. Set to 0% for all-flash arrays with inline dedup.

synology.shr: Synology Hybrid RAID (SHR) provides 1-drive fault tolerance with mixed-size drive support. SHR-2 provides 2-drive fault tolerance.
synology.systemPartition: Synology reserves 20-30 GB per disk for system partitions. With many drives, this fixed overhead can become significant.

dell.powerFlex: PowerFlex supports 2-way and 3-way mirrors with Fine Granularity (8KB) or Medium Granularity (1MB). Fine Granularity adds ~12-15% metadata overhead but enables faster rebuilds.
dell.powerStore: PowerStore is an all-flash appliance with integrated deduplication and compression. Controller limits determine maximum throughput.
dell.powerScale: PowerScale (Isilon) is a scale-out NAS. Efficiency depends on protection level and node count.
dell.objectScale: ObjectScale provides S3-compatible object storage. Multi-site geo-replication can add significant capacity overhead (up to 2.67x for 2-site EC 12+4).
